channel.title: World
channel.description: The Washington Post World section provides information and analysis of breaking world news stories. In addition to our world news and video, Post World News offers discussions and blogs on major international news and economic issues.

item.title: U.S. says it hit targets in Iran as hostilities erupt over Hormuz for third day
item.dc:creator: Victoria Craw, Suzan Haidamous
item.description: Tehran said it launched strikes on U.S. interests in the Middle East, and Bahrain and Kuwait said they were targeted, as clashes continue to threaten the ceasefire.

item.title: These homes rose out of Venezuela’s socialist revolution. Now they’re rubble.
item.dc:creator: Helena Carpio, Samantha Schmidt
item.description: In the absence of government help, ordinary Venezuelans dug barehanded through the rubble of the week’s earthquakes for loved ones.

item.title: Unease deepens in Russia as Ukraine steps up long-range strikes
item.dc:creator: Catherine Belton, Natalia Abbakumova
item.description: Experts said Vladimir Putin was unlikely to change course despite worsening fuel shortages and a sharp decline in the stock market.

item.title: Ukrainian drones drive Russia to declare emergency in occupied Crimea
item.dc:creator: Mary Ilyushina
item.description: Weeks of punishing strikes have disrupted water, fuel and electricity supplies across the peninsula, which Ukraine’s president has vowed to reclaim.

item.title: When it comes to soccer, there is no such thing as a United Kingdom
item.dc:creator: Steve Hendrix
item.description: In this year's World Cup, Scotland’s second favorite team is anyone playing England.

item.title: In AI race vs. U.S., China eyes a come-from-behind victory
item.dc:creator: Rebecca Tan, Lyric Li
item.description: U.S. AI companies seem to be in the lead, but that could be short-lived as Chinese competitors offer cheaper products with more commercial appeal worldwide.

item.title: From foes to ‘great friends’? Quakes test new U.S.-Venezuela relationship.
item.dc:creator: Terrence McCoy
item.description: The country once rejected U.S. aid after a natural disaster. This time, it’s different.
